title: U.S. Army Attack Helicopters Hold Maritime Attack Drills in the Pacific
description: Pacific-based U.S. Army Apache attack helicopters recently rehearsed maritime missions in preparation for regional deployments to upcoming exercises across the first island chain. text:
Pacific-based U.S. Army Apache attack helicopters recently rehearsed maritime missions in preparation for regional deployments to upcoming exercises across the first island chain.
Photos released last week by the 25th Combat Aviation Brigade detailed the Hawaii-based AH-64s practicing “maritime deep attack” scenarios, a mission set that Army aviation has been orienting toward amid an increased demand for long-range aerial assets that could play a role in a conflict with China.
Originally designed to combat massed Soviet mechanized formations in Central Europe, the Apache has seen combat from Panama to Gaza. With lessons in Ukraine and Iran, the Army has eyed new roles for the attack helicopter as a dedicated drone hunter and to bolster American forces operating in the first island chain.
Army rotary-wing aviation also made headlines last month during the raid to capture Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro, which was facilitated by scores of U.S. Navy and Air Force fighters, electronic warfare jets, support aircraft and bombers.
The 25th Combat Aviation Brigade has frequently deployed under the 25th Infantry Division to allies and partners in the Western Pacific under the service’s joint Pacific multinational readiness center efforts, an initiative that deploys American troops to train host nation forces and acclimate to areas they may find themselves in during a conflict with China.
Apaches from the brigade flew to Batanes, a Philippine archipelago located 140 kilometers from Taiwan, during exercise Balikatan 2025. Their presence followed a trend of the ground branch’s aviation capabilities at strategic maritime areas across the Southeast Asian nation. Apaches are expected to join this year’s Balikatan, with several helicopters being loaded onto American logistics ships delivering equipment for the drills.
The Army has also highlighted the capabilities of other Apache-equipped brigades, specifically demonstrating rapid insertion abilities across the Indo-Pacific via C-17 transport aircraft and new Spike non-line-of-sight (NLOS) missiles.
“The Spike NLOS missile’s successful integration on the AH-64E Apache emphasizes the system’s capability to meet evolving mission needs, particularly in maritime and beyond-line-of-sight scenarios,” reads an Army release from August 2025 on an exercise in Poland.
U.S. Marine Corps attack helicopters are also set to receive their own missile for maritime strike scenarios, albeit at a much longer range compared to the Army’s 50-kilometer Spike missiles. Last month, the Marine Corps fast tracked a missile capable of being carried by AH-1Z Vipers that could strike warships more than 200 nautical miles away. With these new strike capabilities, American helicopters can become highly mobile anti-ship assets.
